---
title: "Computation Lab: Basis and Column Space Geometry"
description: "An interactive lab for seeing how different generating sets can define the same subspace and how basis size tracks dimension."
page-type: computation-lab
status: published
update-status: current
level: foundation
topic: linear-algebra
prerequisites:
  - /topics/foundation/linear-algebra/concepts/subspaces-basis-dimension.qmd
learning-goals:
  - "See when a generating set is redundant."
  - "Watch a column space stay the same while the spanning list changes."
  - "Connect determinant-based independence to basis size in two dimensions."
applications:
  - dimensionality-reduction
  - regression
keywords:
  - computation
  - simulation
  - visualization
  - basis
  - subspace
maintainer: mtuann
last-reviewed: 2026-04-24
source-check-date: 2026-04-24
review-cadence: semiannual
date-modified: last-modified
draft: false
execution-engine: ojs
interactive: ojs
reproducibility-note: "Client-side Observable JS only; deterministic generators and slider-controlled angles."
---

## Lab Goal

This lab helps you see one specific fact:

changing the generating set does not necessarily change the subspace, but changing independence changes the basis size.

## Math Question

How do the angle between two main generators and the choice of a third redundant vector affect:

- the dimension of the column space
- whether a basis needs one or two vectors
- whether adding another column changes the underlying subspace

## Model or Setup

We use three column vectors in $\mathbb{R}^2$:

$$
c_1 =
\begin{bmatrix}
1 \\
0
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad
c_2 =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos \theta \\
\sin \theta
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad
c_3 = c_1 + c_2.
$$

The third vector is always in the span of the first two.

## Parameters and Controls

- `Angle`: controls whether $c_1$ and $c_2$ are dependent or independent
- `a, b, d`: coefficients in the combination $a c_1 + b c_2 + d c_3$

## Code and Simulation

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof angleDeg = Inputs.range([0, 180], {
  value: 60,
  step: 1,
  label: "Angle between c1 and c2 (degrees)"
})

viewof aCoef = Inputs.range([-2, 2], {
  value: 1,
  step: 0.05,
  label: "Coefficient a"
})

viewof bCoef = Inputs.range([-2, 2], {
  value: 0.5,
  step: 0.05,
  label: "Coefficient b"
})

viewof dCoef = Inputs.range([-2, 2], {
  value: 0.8,
  step: 0.05,
  label: "Coefficient d"
})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
theta = angleDeg * Math.PI / 180
c1 = ({x: 1, y: 0})
c2 = ({x: Math.cos(theta), y: Math.sin(theta)})
c3 = ({x: c1.x + c2.x, y: c1.y + c2.y})

target = ({
  x: aCoef * c1.x + bCoef * c2.x + dCoef * c3.x,
  y: aCoef * c1.y + bCoef * c2.y + dCoef * c3.y
})

detValue = c1.x * c2.y - c1.y * c2.x
dimensionText =
  Math.abs(detValue) < 1e-6
    ? "Dimension 1: c1 and c2 are dependent, so every column stays on one line."
    : "Dimension 2: c1 and c2 are independent, so c3 adds no new dimension."

segments = [
  {x1: 0, y1: 0, x2: c1.x, y2: c1.y, kind: "c1"},
  {x1: 0, y1: 0, x2: c2.x, y2: c2.y, kind: "c2"},
  {x1: 0, y1: 0, x2: c3.x, y2: c3.y, kind: "c3"},
  {x1: 0, y1: 0, x2: target.x, y2: target.y, kind: "target"}
]
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  height: 430,
  x: {label: "x", domain: [-4, 4]},
  y: {label: "y", domain: [-4, 4]},
  color: {legend: true},
  marks: [
    Plot.gridX({strokeOpacity: 0.15}),
    Plot.gridY({strokeOpacity: 0.15}),
    Plot.ruleX([0], {strokeOpacity: 0.25}),
    Plot.ruleY([0], {strokeOpacity: 0.25}),
    Plot.link(segments, {
      x1: "x1",
      y1: "y1",
      x2: "x2",
      y2: "y2",
      stroke: "kind",
      strokeWidth: 3,
      markerEnd: "arrow"
    }),
    Plot.dot([{x: target.x, y: target.y}], {x: "x", y: "y", fill: "#dc2626", r: 6})
  ]
})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
md`
- Determinant of [c1 c2]: **${detValue.toFixed(4)}**
- Basis interpretation: **${dimensionText}**
- Built vector: **(${target.x.toFixed(3)}, ${target.y.toFixed(3)})**
- Note: c3 is always redundant once c1 and c2 are independent.
`
```

## What To Observe

- When the angle is not `0` or `180`, the first two columns already span all of $\mathbb{R}^2$.
- The third vector changes the spanning list but not the subspace.
- When the angle collapses, all three columns fall into one one-dimensional subspace.
- Dimension tracks independent directions, not the raw number of columns you wrote down.

## Interpretation

This lab shows why basis is a minimal spanning set.

You can keep adding vectors to a generating list, but if they do not add a new independent direction, the subspace does not grow. That is the conceptual heart of basis reduction.

## Failure Modes and Numerical Cautions

- Near-zero determinants can make dependence look visually ambiguous.
- In higher dimensions, basis extraction is less visual and more algebraic, but the same rule still applies.
- A larger generating set is not automatically a richer model if it adds no new directions.

## Reproducibility Notes

- execution engine: Observable JS
- randomness: none
- libraries: Quarto OJS with Plot and Inputs
- render mode: interactive client-side

## Extensions

- replace $c_3$ by another combination of $c_1$ and $c_2$
- move to a 3D example where the ambient dimension is larger than the subspace dimension

## Sources and Further Reading

- [MIT 18.06SC: Basis and Dimension](https://live.ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-06sc-linear-algebra-fall-2011/resources/basis-and-dimension-1/) - `First pass` - official anchor for span, basis, and dimension. Checked `2026-04-24`.
- [Hefferon, Linear Algebra](https://hefferon.net/linearalgebra/) - `Second pass` - useful for more basis-reduction exercises. Checked `2026-04-24`.
- [A Survey: Potential Dimensionality Reduction Methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.11036) - `Paper bridge` - current reminder that low-dimensional modeling starts with honest basis and dimension language. Checked `2026-04-24`.
